mechanismDescription: Chronic hyperinsulinemia → sustained PI3K-AKT → TSC1/2 phosphorylation (inhibited) → Rheb-GTP accumulates → mTORC1 disinhibited on lysosomal membrane
mTORC1 directly phosphorylates TFEB at Ser211 and Ser142. pSer211 creates 14-3-3 binding site → cytoplasmic retention. Rapamycin/rapalogs inhibit mTORC1 allosterically via FKBP12.
pSer211-TFEB binds 14-3-3 → cytoplasmic retention → cannot enter nucleus → CLEAR network genes not transcribed
↓v-ATPase subunits, ↓LAMP proteins, ↓cathepsins → reduced lysosome biogenesis and functional capacity
mTORC1 phosphorylates ULK1 at Ser757, which blocks the AMPK binding site on ULK1
ULK1 pSer757 blocks AMPK binding → AMPK cannot phosphorylate/activate ULK1 at Ser317/Ser777 → autophagy/mitophagy initiation blocked
Damage rate exceeds clearance rate → damaged mitochondria accumulate. Stock-flow relationship
mTORC1 phosphorylates S6K1 at Thr389 (hydrophobic motif), enabling full activation
S6K1 phosphorylates IRS-1 at multiple serine residues: S307, S312, S527, S616, S636/639. Creates 14-3-3 binding sites, promotes degradation
IRS-1 serine phosphorylation: (1) reduces tyrosine phosphorylation by insulin receptor, (2) promotes IRS-1 degradation, (3) creates 14-3-3 binding → sequestration
mTORC1 via S6K1 phosphorylates AMPK at Ser345, preventing its lysosomal recruitment and full activation
Phospho-inhibited AMPK cannot activate ULK1; ULK1 remains phosphorylated/inhibited by mTORC1
SIRT1 activates LKB1 via deacetylation, which activates AMPK at Thr172, leading to TSC2 activation and mTORC1 inhibition
SIRT1 promotes autophagy by deacetylating autophagy proteins and activating AMPK-ULK1 axis
Reduced degradation capacity (↓cathepsins, ↓v-ATPase, high pH) → cargo builds up. Stock-flow: inflow unchanged, outflow reduced
Incomplete degradation of proteins/lipids → oxidation → cross-linking → lipofuscin granules (irreversible)
Lipofuscin directly destabilizes lysosomal membrane through physical/oxidative damage → permeabilization
Lysosomal membrane permeabilization → cathepsins (including active cathepsin B) leak into cytosol
Cytosolic cathepsin B promotes NLRP3-ASC complex assembly
PINK1 stabilizes on depolarized mitochondria → Parkin recruited → ubiquitin chains → autophagy receptors → autophagosome formation
Autophagosome fuses with lysosome via SNARE proteins (STX17, SNAP29, VAMP8)
DNase II requires pH <5 for activity. Lysosomal pH >5.5 → DNase II inactive → mtDNA persists undegraded in autolysosome
Undegraded mtDNA exits through compromised autolysosomal membrane (LMP affects autolysosomes too)
Cytosolic mtDNA (CpG-rich, bacterial-like) sensed by cGAS → 2\
Damaged mitochondria have dysfunctional ETC → electron leak at Complex I/III → superoxide production
ROS oxidizes guanine bases in mtDNA → 8-oxo-deoxyguanosine. mtDNA vulnerable due to proximity to ETC and lack of histones
ROS damages Ca²⁺ handling proteins (MCU, NCLX) → impaired Ca²⁺ efflux + sustained MCU-mediated uptake → matrix Ca²⁺ accumulation
Ca²⁺ binds to mPTP regulatory site (likely CypD) → conformational change → pore opening in inner mitochondrial membrane
mPTP opening causes inner membrane stress + mtDNA interaction with VDAC → triggers VDAC oligomerization in outer membrane
FEN1 endonuclease cleaves ox-mtDNA into 500-650 bp fragments; these fragments exit via mPTP/VDAC pores
VDAC oligomers form large pores in outer membrane → allow 500-650 bp ox-mtDNA fragments to exit
Oxidized mtDNA (8-oxo-dG containing) specifically binds NLRP3 LRR domain and triggers inflammasome assembly. Non-oxidized mtDNA does NOT activate NLRP3
Cytosolic dsDNA (including non-oxidized mtDNA) is sensed by cGAS → 2\
Functional PINK1/Parkin mitophagy clears damaged mitochondria BEFORE mtDNA can escape → prevents mtDNA release
NLRP3 recruits ASC → ASC oligomerizes → recruits pro-caspase-1 → caspase-1 auto-activates
Caspase-1 cleaves pro-IL-1β (31 kDa) to mature IL-1β (17 kDa)
cGAS produces 2\
STING → TBK1 → IRF3 phosphorylation → IRF3 dimerization → nuclear translocation → IFN-α/β transcription
NLRP3 signaling activates GSK-3β (tau kinase)
NLRP3 signaling inhibits PP2A (major tau phosphatase)

GSK-3β phosphorylates tau at multiple pathological sites: pSer199, pSer202/Thr205 (AT8), pThr231, pSer396/Ser404 (PHF-1). Priming at Ser-404 enables phosphorylation cascade. Lithium inhibits GSK-3β competitively vs Mg²⁺.
PP2A is the major tau phosphatase; reduced activity → phospho-tau accumulates
Aβ oligomers are phagocytosed by microglia → lysosomal rupture → NLRP3 activation
Tau aggregates also activate NLRP3 (creates feedforward loop)
Caspase-1 cleaves pro-IL-18 to mature IL-18; IL-18 promotes IFN-γ and enhances neuroinflammation
IL-18 induces IFN-γ production and amplifies inflammatory cascades; elevated in AD brain and CSF
Type I interferon signaling (from STING, mtDNA) induces interferon-stimulated genes; ISG signature elevated in AD microglia
ISGs drive microglial activation and proinflammatory phenotype; IRM (interferon-responsive microglia) are distinct subtype
Cytokines and DAMPs activate microglia from homeostatic state
Microglial activation triggers NF-κB nuclear translocation
NF-κB signaling stabilizes HIF-1α under normoxia
HIF-1α → PKM2 dimer → aerobic glycolysis (Warburg effect)
Glycolytic intermediates activate SREBP1 lipogenic program
SREBP1 drives lipid synthesis → lipid droplet formation
Lipid droplet accumulation defines LDAM phenotype
LDAM phenotype characterized by defective phagocytosis
LDAM microglia produce C1q
Activated microglia produce IL-1α
Activated microglia produce TNF-α
IL-1α required for A1 astrocyte induction (with TNF + C1q)
TNF required for A1 astrocyte induction (with IL-1α + C1q)
C1q required for A1 astrocyte induction (with IL-1α + TNF)
mTORC1 stabilizes HIF-1α via increased translation (4E-BP1 phosphorylation) and decreased degradation (reduced PHD activity). This occurs INDEPENDENT of hypoxia in inflammatory microglia.
mTOR-HIF1α axis drives glycolytic program: HIF-1α → LDHA↑, HK2↑, PDK1↑ → pyruvate → lactate instead of TCA cycle. PKM2 dimerization favors biosynthesis over energy.
NF-κB → IRG1 (ACOD1) transcription. IRG1 diverts aconitate from TCA cycle to produce itaconate. Key metabolic adaptation to chronic inflammation.
Itaconate competitively inhibits SDH (Complex II) → succinate accumulates → pseudohypoxic signaling → HIF-1α stabilization (positive feedback to glycolytic switch).
SDH is TCA cycle Complex II. Inhibition breaks the cycle at succinate → fumarate step. Combined with aconitate diversion to itaconate, TCA cycle cannot function normally.
Broken TCA cycle requires anaplerotic inputs. Glutaminolysis provides α-ketoglutarate to "restart" the cycle. SDH-deficient cells show 75% of succinate from glutamine (vs 32% WT).
Glutamine → glutamate (glutaminase) releases NH₃. Glutamate → α-KG (GDH) releases another NH₃. Net: 0.5-1 mol ammonia per mol glutamine. Basic biochemistry.
Ammonia → astrocyte glutamine synthetase → glutamine accumulation → osmotic swelling → clasmatodendrosis cascade. Links microglial metabolic collapse to astrocyte endfoot dysfunction.
LDAM phenotype + iron loading + lysosomal dysfunction = "super-seeder" state. Iron impairs lysosomal function, worsening Aβ aggregation instead of clearance.

PARADIGM SHIFT (Baligács 2024): Microglia don't clear Aβ—they SEED plaques. When microglia depleted: insoluble Aβ (plaques) DECREASED but soluble Aβ UNCHANGED. Proves microglia convert soluble → insoluble.
BACE1 cleaves APP at β-site → releases sAPPβ and membrane-bound β-CTF (C99)
γ-secretase cleaves β-CTF → releases Aβ peptides (Aβ40, Aβ42, Aβ43)
Aβ monomers aggregate into soluble oligomers (dimers, trimers, Aβ*56). Oligomers are the TOXIC species. Aβ CNS half-life ~9h (age 30: 3.8h → age 80: 9.4h), synthesis 7.6%/h, clearance 8.3%/h.
Oligomers seed fibril formation → amyloid plaques. Plaques may be PROTECTIVE sequestration of toxic oligomers
Aβ oligomers bind PrPc/mGluR5 → block LTP, enhance LTD → synaptic dysfunction
Impaired microglial phagocytosis (from LDAM phenotype) reduces Aβ clearance rate
Lysosomal de-acidification → autolysosomes become Aβ production sites (PANTHOS). Aβ produced INSIDE neurons, not just secreted
Amyloidogenic processing of APP by BACE1 produces β-CTF (C99) substrate for γ-secretase
BACE1 upregulation shifts APP processing toward amyloidogenic pathway vs. non-amyloidogenic α-secretase cleavage
Amyloidogenic processing pathway generates Aβ peptides through sequential BACE1 and γ-secretase cleavage
Aβ production rate determines steady-state monomer concentration; monomers aggregate into toxic oligomers
TREM2-positive microglia compact plaques into dense cores, reducing toxicity by sequestering oligomers
Compacted plaques form physical barrier that limits diffusion of toxic Aβ oligomers to surrounding neuropil
Aβ oligomers bind synaptic receptors (PrPc, mGluR5, NMDAR) triggering excitotoxicity and LTP inhibition
Aβ binding to PrPc/mGluR5 complex activates Fyn kinase → NMDAR overactivation → LTP blocked, LTD enhanced
Aβ oligomers activate classical complement cascade → C1q/C3 tag synapses → microglia CR3-mediated pruning. Synapse loss occurs BEFORE plaques form.

Hyperphosphorylation promotes tau detachment from microtubules and conformational changes exposing aggregation-prone regions. Wild-type tau t½ ~16h; phosphomimetic tau t½ >24h; autophagy is primary clearance pathway.
Misfolded tau forms paired helical filaments (PHF) → neurofibrillary tangles (NFT)
Aggregated tau acts as seed, templating misfolding of normal tau in connected neurons
Tau aggregates disrupt axonal transport, impair proteostasis, cause synaptic dysfunction
CSE catalyzes cysteine → H₂S + pyruvate. CSE is the primary H₂S synthase in brain
H₂S sulfhydrates GSK3β at Cys218 → inhibits kinase activity → reduces tau phosphorylation
CSE provides cysteine via transsulfuration; cysteine is rate-limiting for glutathione (GSH) synthesis
CSE expression/activity declines with age → H₂S and cysteine depletion
IL-1β and TNF-α from neuroinflammation activate p38 MAPK, a tau kinase
p38 MAPK directly phosphorylates tau at multiple AD-relevant epitopes (Ser396, Ser404)
Aβ oligomers inhibit PP2A via SET/I2PP2A upregulation; PP2A is the major tau phosphatase (>70%)
Reduced PP2A activity allows hyperphosphorylated tau to accumulate
Aggregated tau assembles into paired helical filaments (PHF) as canonical aggregation product
PHF bundles accumulate into neurofibrillary tangles (NFT); Braak staging correlates with cognitive decline
NFT-bearing neurons show impaired function; tau pathology more closely correlates with dementia than amyloid
Tau is released via exosomes; exosomal tau is more aggregation-prone and seeds misfolding in recipient cells
Exosome-delivered tau seeds misfolding in connected neurons; mediates Braak-like spreading
Homocysteine is substrate for CBS (cystathionine β-synthase); elevated homocysteine is AD risk factor
CBS converts homocysteine + serine → cystathionine (first step of transsulfuration)
Cystathionine is substrate for CSE (cystathionine γ-lyase)
CSE cleaves cystathionine → cysteine + α-ketobutyrate + NH₃
Cysteine is rate-limiting substrate for glutathione (GSH) synthesis
H₂S mediates protein S-sulfhydration (persulfidation), a post-translational modification affecting ~20% of proteins
Sulfhydration of GSK3β at Cys218 inhibits its kinase activity
Sulfhydrated GSK3β is inactive → reduced tau phosphorylation at Ser396, Ser404

Anti-IgLON5 autoantibodies bind IgLON5 → surface clustering + KCNA2 (Kv1.2) co-clustering → altered ion channel function → increased neuronal firing rate. Proven by Ca imaging and MEA in primary neurons. In vivo (mouse ICV): 33% astrocytosis, 100% microgliosis.

Neuronal hyperactivity causes tau to mislocalize from axon to somatodendritic compartment. CRITICAL: Dampening activity with TTX or CNQX PREVENTED tau missorting, proving hyperactivity is UPSTREAM, not downstream, of tau pathology. Mechanism: Activity-dependent Ca influx may trigger tau release from microtubules.

Somatodendritic tau is exposed to kinases concentrated in soma (GSK3β, p38-MAPK). IgLON5 model shows AT8, PHF-1 epitope phosphorylation (pSer396/404) follows missorting. Temporal sequence: hyperactivity → missorting → hyperphosphorylation → aggregation.

C1q increases ~300-fold in aged brain (primarily from microglia). MECHANISM UNKNOWN per Stephan 2013. Likely intermediates: microglial senescence, oxidative stress, or myelin debris accumulation. Reactivation of developmental pruning pathway.
C1q binds synapses → activates C1r/C1s → cleaves C4 → cleaves C2 → cleaves C3 → deposits iC3b
C3 cleavage deposits iC3b on synapses → recognized by microglial CR3 (CD11b/CD18)
CR3 binding to iC3b-tagged synapses triggers microglial phagocytosis → synapse elimination
Excessive synapse engulfment → synapse loss → circuit dysfunction
Synapse density is STRONGEST anatomical correlate of cognitive function - better than plaques or tangles
Aβ oligomers induce C1q expression/deposition on synapses
Aging causes dramatic C1q elevation (~300-fold); c1q_elevated captures sustained high levels beyond normal aging. MECHANISM UNKNOWN - likely involves microglial senescence or debris accumulation.
Sustained elevated C1q drives excessive complement cascade activation and synapse tagging
Aβ oligomers reduce BDNF expression and secretion. Involves CREB inhibition and impaired TrkB signaling. Contributes to synaptic dysfunction.
BDNF is essential for LTP, spine formation, and neuronal survival. Signals via TrkB receptor → MAPK/ERK, PI3K/Akt, PLCγ pathways. Master regulator of synaptic plasticity.
BDNF promotes spine formation and stabilization. TrkB signaling activates Rac1/Cdc42 → actin polymerization → spine morphogenesis. Critical for learning-related structural plasticity.
Aβ oligomers cause rapid spine retraction within hours. Involves calcineurin activation, AMPA receptor internalization, and cofilin-mediated actin depolymerization. Early event in AD pathology.
Higher spine density provides more substrate for synaptic plasticity. Mushroom spines represent stable, functional synapses; thin spines are immature and plastic.
IL-6/IL-1β → JAK2 → STAT3 → HAMP promoter → hepcidin transcription. Links neuroinflammation to iron maldistribution
Hepcidin binds ferroportin → internalization → ubiquitination → degradation. Ferroportin is ONLY cellular iron exporter
Ferroportin exports Fe²⁺ from cells. When degraded, iron accumulates intracellularly
Fe²⁺ + H₂O₂ → Fe³⁺ + OH• + OH⁻ (Fenton reaction). Hydroxyl radicals attack membrane PUFA → lipid peroxides
GPX4 reduces lipid peroxides (LOOH → LOH) using GSH. GPX4 is the ONLY enzyme that detoxifies membrane lipid peroxides
Accumulated lipid peroxides → membrane rupture → ferroptotic cell death
GSH is essential cofactor for GPX4. GSH depletion → GPX4 inactive → ferroptosis
Chronological aging drives accumulation of senescent cells through replicative exhaustion, oxidative damage, and iron accumulation
Ferroportin degradation blocks iron export → total brain iron increases despite cytosolic iron deficiency
Accumulated iron is stored in ferritin cages (Fe³⁺); ferritin increases to detoxify iron but TRAPS it
De-acidified lysosomes cannot release iron from ferritin (requires low pH); iron becomes trapped in lysosomes
Iron locked in ferritin → cytosolic labile iron pool depleted → functional iron deficiency despite total iron increase
Iron trapped in lysosomes cannot be released → functional iron deficiency in cytosol
Counterintuitively, functional iron deficiency (low labile Fe²⁺) may initially PROTECT from ferroptosis, but impairs mitochondrial function
Iron accumulation directly drives cellular senescence; iron chelation reverses senescent phenotype

Excitotoxicity drives excitatory neuron senescence: 1. Chronic NMDAR activation → calcium overload 2. Mitochondrial stress → DNA damage 3. p53/p21 activation → senescence program Lund 2026 (Cell): Excitatory neuron senescence → VOLUME LOSS. Integrates with Gabitto 2024: excitatory neurons hyperactive BEFORE dying.

Lund et al. Cell 2026 (Living Brain Project): - ALL senescence-volume correlations NEGATIVE for excitatory neurons - Higher senescence → SMALLER brain volumes - Conserved from early development through aging - OPPOSITE pattern to microglia (which show positive correlation) This explains volume loss in AD and GWI without requiring cell death.
Lund et al. Cell 2026: - CREB5 negatively correlated with microglial senescence - CREB5 normally reduces oxidative stress and promotes plasticity - **CREB5 is DECREASED in Alzheimer
CREB5 expression declines with age and in AD. Lund 2026: CREB5 is a key regulator of microglial senescence. Age-related CREB5 decline may contribute to AD vulnerability.

PARADOX: Microglial senescence shows OPPOSITE volume relationship. Lund 2026: 23/25 correlations POSITIVE (more senescence → larger volume). Interpretation: Senescent microglia may help maintain brain structure, possibly through reduced inflammatory activity or support functions. This contrasts sharply with excitatory neurons where senescence → atrophy.
APOE4 Arg112 causes domain interaction between N- and C-terminus → unstable, poorly lipidated
Domain interaction impairs ABCA1-mediated lipidation → hypolipidated APOE4
REST nuclear localization declines with age in vulnerable neurons
REST coordinates with Nrf2 in stress resistance program
Nrf2 induces GPX4 and other ferroptosis-protective genes
APOE4 impairs cholesterol trafficking → cholesterol trapped in endo-lysosomes (NPC-like phenotype)
Cholesterol accumulation in lysosomes impairs lysosomal function and autophagy
APOE4 impairs lipid transport → lipid droplet accumulation in astrocytes
Lipid-laden astrocytes contribute to neuroinflammation via altered lipid signaling
Aging leads to nuclear REST depletion in vulnerable neurons; more severe in AD
REST depletion de-represses apoptotic/neurodegenerative genes; neurons lose stress resistance
ApoE4 transports sulfatides more efficiently than E3 or E2. Sulfatide content in ApoE-HDL: E4 > E3 > E2 (Han 2010). This is a "too much of a good thing" problem.
Enhanced sulfatide transport by ApoE4 → excessive removal of sulfatides from oligodendrocytes/myelin → sulfatide depletion
Progressive sulfatide loss reaches pathological threshold → sulfatide-depleted state detectable at MCI
Sulfatide depletion compromises myelin sheath stability, paranodal junction formation, and ion channel clustering
Loss of myelin integrity → axo-glial junction disruption → demyelination → axonal dysfunction
Myelin breakdown stresses oligodendrocytes; compensatory remyelination attempts fail under chronic sulfatide depletion
Dysfunctional oligodendrocytes produce less OMG (oligodendrocyte myelin glycoprotein)
Brain OMG levels reflected in plasma. Low plasma OMG predicts dementia 7-20 years before diagnosis.
Brain sulfatide levels reflected in CSF. CSF sulfatide/PI ratio decreases at MCI stage.
Sulfatides enhance Aβ binding to ApoE-containing particles and facilitate Aβ clearance via endocytosis. Sulfatide depletion impairs this clearance pathway.
Protective APOE variants (E2, E3-Jacksonville V236E) preserve brain sulfatides. E3-Jacksonville increases sulfatides.
TREM2 signaling required for transition from homeostatic to DAM phenotype
Stage 1 DAM → Stage 2 DAM requires TREM2 signaling
DAM microglia form protective barrier around plaques, compacting them to reduce toxicity
R47H and other AD-risk variants reduce TREM2 surface expression/signaling
ADAM10/17 cleave TREM2 ectodomain → soluble TREM2 (sTREM2) in CSF
TREM2 loss-of-function variants block Stage 1 → Stage 2 DAM transition, locking microglia in dysfunctional state
Blocked DAM transition prevents microglia from acquiring phagocytic phenotype needed for Aβ clearance
Aging promotes senescent TREM2+ microglia with SASP-like phenotype; these may be harmful
Senescent TREM2+ microglia secrete inflammatory factors and fail to maintain tissue homeostasis
DAM Stage 2 microglia have enhanced phagocytic capacity (TREM2-dependent). DAM upregulate lipid metabolism genes (APOE, LIPA, LPL) and phagocytic receptors to clear debris.
APOE4 fails to suppress CypA in pericytes → CypA accumulates → MMP9 activation → BBB breakdown
CypA → NF-κB → MMP9 transcription and activation
MMP9 degrades basement membrane and tight junction proteins → BBB leakage
Glymphatic clearance is 60% higher during sleep. Sleep disruption impairs Aβ clearance
Glymphatic system clears Aβ and other waste; impairment → Aβ accumulation
Glymphatic function declines 80-90% with age (AQP4 redistribution, arterial stiffening)
MMP9 damages pericytes; sPDGFRβ in CSF is biomarker of pericyte injury
Pericyte loss disrupts BBB integrity; pericytes essential for BBB maintenance
Meningeal lymphatic function declines with age; impairs CNS waste drainage
Meningeal lymphatics drain glymphatic efflux; lymphatic dysfunction impairs overall clearance
Glymphatic flow drives ISF Aβ clearance via perivascular drainage
Efficient ISF Aβ clearance reduces oligomer accumulation
AQP4 water channels at astrocyte endfeet enable CSF-ISF exchange. TGN-020 (AQP4 inhibitor) blocks 40Hz-mediated amyloid clearance. AQP4 knockout eliminates exercise benefits
CSF-ISF exchange is the core mechanism of glymphatic clearance. CSF influx via periarterial spaces, waste-laden ISF efflux via perivenous spaces
Aging disrupts DAPC anchoring → AQP4 redistributes from endfeet to parenchymal membrane. AQP4 polarization ratio declines with age, contributing to 80-90% glymphatic decline
AQP4 depolarization impairs CSF-ISF exchange → reduced glymphatic clearance. DTNB (β-dystrobrevin) is an AD risk gene that encodes AQP4 anchoring complex component
Arterial pulsatility (vasomotion) drives CSF influx along periarterial spaces. Two-photon imaging shows 40Hz increases high-amplitude vasomotor events. Arterial stiffening with age reduces flow
Aerobic exercise (swimming, running) upregulates Lama1 and Dp71 transcription → restores DAPC → restores AQP4 polarization at endfeet. Effect requires intact AQP4
Exercise increases cardiac output → increased arterial pulsatility → enhanced perivascular CSF flow. Converges with 40Hz gamma stimulation (VIP-mediated pulsatility)
Sleep deprivation impairs AQP4 polarization maintenance. AQP4 SNPs moderate the relationship between sleep and brain Aβ burden in humans

Astrocyte endfeet provide the structural scaffold for AQP4 polarization via the DAPC complex (Laminin → Dystroglycan → Dystrophin → Syntrophin → AQP4). Endfoot retraction or damage causes AQP4 depolarization

Astrocyte endfeet ensheath >99% of cerebral blood vessels and support BBB integrity via: (1) Laminin/dystroglycan signaling to pericytes; (2) Release of sonic hedgehog (SHH) supporting tight junctions; (3) Physical barrier limiting perivascular infiltration
Astrocyte endfeet form the glymphatic "gate" enabling CSF-ISF exchange at the perivascular space. AQP4 on endfeet mediates water flux; endfoot swelling/retraction alters perivascular space geometry

A1 reactive astrocyte conversion causes endfoot retraction and structural damage. A1 astrocytes lose normal astrocyte functions including endfoot maintenance, AQP4 polarization, and BBB support. C3 complement released by A1s may damage endfeet

Astrocyte endfeet contain lysosomes that process perivascular debris and Aβ. Endfoot damage impairs local lysosomal clearance capacity. Astrocyte lysosomes also process material cleared via glymphatic system

Astrocyte endfeet express α7 nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (nAChRs). Cholinergic signaling via α7 nAChRs supports endfoot function and BBB integrity through the splenic anti-inflammatory pathway (reduced systemic TNF-α/IL-1β → preserved tight junctions)
Aging causes astrocyte endfoot retraction from blood vessels, contributing to BBB breakdown and AQP4 depolarization. DAPC components decline with age, loosening AQP4 anchoring
Pericyte injury releases soluble PDGFRβ into CSF. This is the EARLIEST known biomarker of AD pathology, detectable ~45 years before symptom onset in APOE4 carriers.

LRP1 is the primary receptor for Aβ clearance at the BBB. ApoE4 reduces LRP1 expression and function, leading to impaired Aβ transcytosis from brain to blood. This establishes BBB dysfunction as UPSTREAM of amyloid accumulation.

BBB breakdown reduces Aβ clearance via two mechanisms: (1) reduced LRP1-mediated efflux, (2) peripheral proteins entering brain can nucleate Aβ aggregation. BBB dysfunction is detectable before amyloid PET positivity.
Hyperphosphorylated tau at T217 is actively secreted from neurons and released upon neuronal damage. pTau217 in plasma closely tracks tau PET signal and predicts progression.
pTau181 is released from neurons undergoing tau pathology. Less specific than pTau217 but FDA-cleared as first blood AD biomarker.
Neurofilament light chain (NfL) is released from axons upon neuronal damage. Plasma NfL is a sensitive but non-specific marker of neurodegeneration.
Retinal nerve fiber layer thickness (measured by OCT) reflects retinal ganglion cell loss, which parallels central neurodegeneration. Non-invasive marker.
ISF carrying soluble Aβ drains along basement membranes within arterial walls (IPAD pathway). This is separate from but complementary to glymphatic clearance.
IPAD removes soluble Aβ from brain parenchyma. Impaired in CAA where Aβ deposits in vessel walls. Arterial stiffening with age reduces IPAD efficiency.
Meningeal lymphatics drain CSF and brain-derived antigens to deep cervical lymph nodes. This is where brain Aβ first contacts peripheral immune system.
Aβ and Aβ-antibody complexes from cervical lymph nodes enter systemic circulation and are filtered by splenic macrophages.
Aβ from cervical lymph nodes enters systemic circulation and is cleared by liver Kupffer cells and LRP1 on hepatocytes.
Splenic macrophages clear Aβ-antibody immune complexes from blood. Primary peripheral clearance organ. Splenectomy may reduce efficacy of anti-Aβ immunotherapy.
Liver clears 40-60% of peripheral Aβ via Kupffer cells and LRP1-mediated hepatocyte uptake. Works in parallel with splenic clearance.
Theory: Reducing plasma Aβ creates concentration gradient pulling Aβ from brain. Evidence mixed; equilibrium is slow (weeks) and receptor-limited.
FcRn at BBB primarily effluxes IgG OUT of brain (brain→blood). Anti-Aβ antibodies achieve only ~0.1% brain uptake because FcRn actively pumps them back out.
FcRn salvage pathway extends IgG half-life to ~21 days by rescuing antibodies from lysosomal degradation. Maintains high peripheral antibody concentrations.
Anti-Aβ antibody clearance of CAA (cerebral amyloid angiopathy) triggers complement activation and transient BBB disruption → vasogenic edema. Higher in APOE4 carriers.
Severe ARIA-E can progress to microhemorrhages (ARIA-H) from vessel wall weakening. Risk factors: APOE4/4 genotype, high baseline CAA, anticoagulant use.
Brain amyloid deposition sequesters Aβ42 preferentially over Aβ40, decreasing plasma Aβ42/40 ratio. Lower ratio indicates higher brain amyloid burden.
Aβ deposits in retina mirror brain amyloid burden. Detectable with curcumin fluorescence imaging. Provides non-invasive measure of systemic amyloidosis.
Abnormal tau triggers autoimmune response; anti-MAPT autoantibodies are component of AD autoantibody panel. May reflect early immune recognition of tau pathology.
BBB breakdown impairs LRP1-mediated Aβ transcytosis from brain to blood. Pericyte loss → reduced Aβ clearance across endothelium.
Basal forebrain cholinergic neurons (nbM) are selectively vulnerable to tau pathology
Loss of cholinergic neurons → reduced ACh synthesis and release
ACh is essential for attention, learning, memory. Basis for AChEI therapy
Neuroinflammation damages oligodendrocytes and myelin → white matter hyperintensities on MRI
White matter pathology leads to myelin breakdown and oligodendrocyte loss
Myelin breakdown slows conduction velocity and disrupts neural circuits

OPCs differentiate into mature myelinating oligodendrocytes. Process requires: (1) exit from cell cycle, (2) expression of MBP/MOG/PLP1, (3) cholesterol availability for myelin synthesis. Declines with age due to OPC senescence, LINGO1↑, Nogo receptor activation.

Oligodendrocytes require massive cholesterol for myelin synthesis (myelin is 70% lipid, mostly cholesterol). OLs can synthesize de novo OR receive from astrocytes via APOE. Rate-limiting for remyelination.
Mature oligodendrocytes maintain myelin sheaths. When OLs die (A1 astrocyte toxicity, APOE4 cholesterol failure), myelin degenerates. Loss of mature OLs → myelin breakdown → axonal dysfunction.

APOE4 impairs oligodendrocyte cholesterol via TWO mechanisms: (1) ↓SREBP2 in OLs reduces de novo synthesis, (2) poorly lipidated secreted APOE4 cannot deliver cholesterol from astrocytes. OLs become cholesterol-starved → cannot maintain myelin.
OPCs constitutively secrete TGF-β1 which is required for BBB integrity. OPC-specific TGF-β1 knockout → cerebral hemorrhage in mice.
OPC-derived TGF-β1 activates TGF-βR on pericytes and endothelium → maintains tight junction proteins (claudin-5, occludin, ZO-1). Loss of OPCs → ↓TGF-β1 → BBB breakdown.

OPCs wrap capillaries; Ca²⁺ transients in OPCs PRECEDE pericyte relaxation and vessel dilation during functional hyperemia. OPCs may be upstream mediators of neurovascular coupling, not just responders.

HUMAN-SPECIFIC: Human OPCs express NOS1 (τ=0.85 cell type specificity); mouse OPCs do NOT express Nos1. Human OPC-derived NO may regulate vascular tone through a mechanism absent in mouse models. This represents a critical translational gap.

A1 reactive astrocytes secrete saturated lipids that are toxic to oligodendrocytes. This is one mechanism of white matter pathology in AD. A1 astrocytes are induced by C1q + IL-1α + TNF-α from activated microglia.

OPCs become exhausted and senescent with age. Mechanisms include: (1) replicative senescence, (2) epigenetic changes reducing differentiation capacity, (3) accumulation of myelin debris inhibiting OPC function. Results in declining remyelination capacity.

Remyelination capacity declines with age due to: (1) OPC exhaustion/senescence, (2) ↑LINGO1 inhibiting OPC differentiation, (3) altered macrophage/microglia clearance of myelin debris, (4) cholesterol metabolism changes. This creates a window where demyelination becomes irreversible.

Myelin breakdown leads to detectable white matter pathology on MRI (↓fractional anisotropy, ↑mean diffusivity, white matter hyperintensities). This occurs BEFORE gray matter changes and predicts cognitive decline.

White matter pathology disrupts long-range projections from basal forebrain cholinergic neurons to cortex and hippocampus. The cholinergic system has the longest axons in the brain and is particularly vulnerable to WM damage. This may explain why cholinergic deficits appear early in AD.

Aggregated tau is internalized by microglia, triggering DAP12/TREM2 signaling cascade. DAP12 ITAM phosphorylation → pAKT↑, pERK↑, pNFκB↑, IFN signaling↑. This microglial response initiates the SLIT2 demyelination cascade.

DAP12-activated microglia release unknown signal (likely IL-1β, TNF-α, or complement) → excitatory neurons upregulate SLIT2 transcription via NFκB. SLIT2 is normally a developmental guidance cue; pathological reactivation in adult brain.

Secreted SLIT2 binds ROBO1 Ig1 domain on oligodendrocytes. SLIT2 D2 domain contacts ROBO1 via concave surface of leucine-rich repeat. Crystal structure solved by Morlot 2007 (PMID:17893336). SLIT2 localizes to MBP+ myelinated axons in AD.

SLIT2-ROBO1 binding causes Fyn kinase to dissociate from ROBO1 cytoplasmic domain. Fyn normally maintains OL process extension and promotes differentiation. Fyn dissociation → Fyn DEACTIVATION → loss of pro-myelinating signals.

Active Fyn normally suppresses RhoA-GTP via p190RhoGAP activation. When Fyn is deactivated (by SLIT2-ROBO1 signaling), p190RhoGAP activity decreases → RhoA-GTP INCREASES. Note: edge shows Fyn DECREASES RhoA-GTP (normally); loss of Fyn = increased RhoA-GTP.

RhoA-GTP activates ROCK kinase → phosphorylates MLC → actomyosin contraction → actin stress fibers → OL process RETRACTION from axons. Fasudil (ROCK inhibitor) blocks this but is non-selective for cell types.

OL process retraction causes myelin sheath withdrawal from axons. Physical changes: OL "pulls back" from axon, failed membrane spreading, blocked differentiation, disrupted myelin compaction. This is INSIDE-OUT demyelination (OL withdrawal), not OUTSIDE-IN (immune attack).

Myelin sheath withdrawal leads to myelin breakdown and debris accumulation. Debris then secondarily inhibits OPC differentiation via Nogo receptor, completing the demyelination cycle. Key distinction: the PRIMARY event is withdrawal, breakdown is SECONDARY.

Aβ42 binds α7nAChR with ultra-high affinity (10 picomolar). This was first published by Wang 2000 and represents one of the highest affinity receptor-ligand interactions in biology. The α7nAChR becomes the molecular nexus where soluble Aβ triggers BOTH tau pathology AND intraneuronal amyloid.

Aβ42–α7nAChR complex activates kinases (GSK-3β, CDK5, Fyn) that hyperphosphorylate tau. This mechanistically links the hallmark plaques and tangles through a single receptor interaction. Hyperphosphorylated tau can no longer stabilize microtubules.

As soluble Aβ42 piles onto α7nAChR, the Aβ42–α7nAChR complex is internalized by endocytosis, leading to intraneuronal amyloid aggregates. After neuronal death, these aggregates become extracellular dense-core plaques.

Hyperphosphorylated tau detaches from microtubules, causing microtubule destabilization and impaired intraneuronal transport. This transport failure causes accumulation of proteins including tau itself, creating a vicious cycle.

Impaired intraneuronal transport caused by microtubule destabilization leads to accumulation and aggregation of hyperphosphorylated tau. This completes the mechanistic link from Aβ42–α7nAChR complex to neurofibrillary tangles.

BMP (bis(monoacylglycero)phosphate) is a lysosome-specific lipid that facilitates lipid degradation by activating acid sphingomyelinase. ↑BMP is a compensatory response to lysosomal stress - the cell attempts to enhance lipid catabolism. BMP elevation is seen in lysosomal storage disorders and in AD brains.

Pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-1β increase cellular stress on lysosomes. NF-κB activation downstream of IL-1R impairs TFEB nuclear translocation, reducing lysosomal biogenesis. Chronic inflammation creates a vicious cycle of lysosomal dysfunction.

Type I IFN signaling impairs mitochondrial quality control. ISG expression alters cellular metabolism and reduces PINK1/Parkin-mediated mitophagy efficiency. This completes the mtDNA-STING inflammation loop.
Aβ oligomers trigger microglial activation through multiple receptors including TLR4, RAGE, and scavenger receptors. This completes the LDAM-Aβ clearance failure loop.

Synapse loss releases tonic microglial inhibition. Neurons express CX3CL1 (fractalkine) which binds microglial CX3CR1 to maintain quiescence. Synapse loss reduces this inhibitory signal, promoting microglial activation.
Myelin debris inhibits OPC differentiation through Nogo receptor activation. Chronic demyelination exhausts the OPC pool. This completes the OPC-myelin-white matter degeneration loop.
Intact BBB protects OPCs from peripheral insults. BBB breakdown exposes OPCs to serum factors (fibrinogen, albumin, IgG) that inhibit OPC differentiation and survival.
OLs preferentially accumulate iron due to high metabolic demand for myelination. Iron required for cholesterol synthesis, cytochrome oxidase, and fatty acid desaturation.
GPX4 is the master brake on OL ferroptosis. GPX4 reduces lipid peroxides in OL membranes; when GPX4 is depleted, high-iron OLs undergo ferroptotic death.
High OL iron content + declining GPX4 with age → Fenton chemistry → lipid peroxidation in PUFA-rich myelin membranes → ferroptotic OL death.
OL ferroptotic death → myelin sheath collapse → white matter pathology. This may occur in PARALLEL to or BEFORE A1 astrocyte-mediated OL killing.
Myelin debris activates astrocytes toward A1 phenotype via TLR2/4 signaling. Creates bidirectional amplification loop: A1 astrocytes kill OLs → myelin breakdown → more A1 activation.
PS1/PS2 mutations increase ER-mitochondria contacts (MAM) → enhanced Ca²⁺ transfer
MAM hyperconnectivity → excessive Ca²⁺ transfer from ER to mitochondria via IP3R-VDAC-MCU
Excessive Ca²⁺ transfer → mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload → mPTP opening
γ-secretase is enriched at MAM; MAM expansion increases Aβ production
PS1 mutations cause ER Ca²⁺ leak dysfunction; may cause ER Ca²⁺ overfilling or depletion
ER Ca²⁺ stores drive Ca²⁺ flux to mitochondria via MAM
MAM-mediated Ca²⁺ transfer leads to mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload when dysregulated
MAM-derived Ca²⁺ overload contributes to overall mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload → mPTP opening
CNS exposure required for target engagement. Must verify with PET or CSF biomarkers
Exercise increases glymphatic clearance of Aβ and other waste products
Regular exercise reduces systemic and neuroinflammation via IL-6 myokine signaling
Exercise activates AMPK, inhibits mTORC1, and enhances autophagy flux
Target engagement should produce measurable biomarker changes (PET, CSF, plasma)
Biomarker changes should translate to clinical benefit - but this link is NOT guaranteed
Clinical benefit is measured by cognitive and functional outcomes
Biological sex determines baseline estrogen levels; menopause causes precipitous decline in women
Estrogen provides neuroprotection via anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, neurotrophic effects; loss increases vulnerability
Elevated FSH (post-menopausal) may directly contribute to neurodegeneration via C/EBPβ-δ-secretase pathway
APOE4 risk varies by ancestry and sex: highest in Japanese, intermediate in European, lower in African/Hispanic; women with APOE4 at higher risk
Biological sex determines testosterone levels; higher in males; testosterone may have neuroprotective effects
Testosterone reduces neuroinflammation and Aβ accumulation; low testosterone associated with AD risk in men
X chromosome carries lysosomal genes (ATP6AP2, SLC9A7, ATP6AP1, LAMP2); XX females have dosage compensation but may have advantage
Lysosomal genes on X chromosome contribute to maintaining lysosomal function
Estrogen loss in menopause → increased visceral adipose tissue (VAT); VAT produces inflammatory cytokines
VAT secretes IL-6, TNF-α, leptin → systemic and neuroinflammation; links metabolic syndrome to AD risk
Women with AD show paradoxical negative iron-ferritin correlation (opposite of normal); suggests iron storage mechanism failure
Iron storage failure → increased labile iron → ferroptosis vulnerability; female-specific pathway
Vaccination induces trained immunity: long-term epigenetic reprogramming (H3K4me3, H3K27ac) and metabolic changes in innate immune cells. Both adjuvanted AND non-adjuvanted vaccines work.
Peripheral trained immunity signals reach CNS, reprogramming microglia. Microglia are long-lived and self-renewing, making them ideal substrates for persistent trained immunity.
Trained microglia show altered cytokine profiles, including enhanced IFN-γ signaling, which may enhance clearance capacity
Trained microglia show enhanced phagocytic capacity and improved Aβ clearance, while maintaining regulated (not excessive) inflammatory responses
Lysosomal pH drop to <6.0 triggers autocatalytic activation of AEP (legumain). Pro-form cleaves itself at D25 and D233 to generate mature enzyme.
Active AEP cleaves vimentin at N283, generating fragments that cannot maintain cytoskeletal network structure. This is the key molecular event triggering clasmatodendrosis.
Cleaved vimentin cannot co-polymerize with GFAP. Loss of vimentin-GFAP interaction destabilizes the intermediate filament network, leading to cytoskeletal collapse.
Cytoskeletal collapse removes structural support for astrocyte endfeet → process beading, fragmentation, and retraction from blood vessels (clasmatodendrosis).
Damaged and fragmenting endfeet release GFAP into extracellular space. GFAP then enters CSF and eventually plasma, serving as a biomarker.
Endfoot retraction creates gap between blood vessel and glia limitans → perivascular space enlarges → visible on MRI as white matter hyperintensities around vessels.
Chronic sleep disruption increases lysosomal stress and AEP activity. Sleep deprivation also elevates C/EBPβ, which transcriptionally upregulates AEP.
Chronic cerebral hypoperfusion activates C/EBPβ → transcriptional upregulation of AEP gene (LGMN) → increased AEP protein and activity.
Endfoot fragmentation disrupts DAPC anchoring complex → AQP4 redistributes from endfeet to parenchymal membrane → loss of polarized water transport.
A1 reactive astrocytes have increased lysosomal stress and inflammatory signaling, which upregulates AEP activity via C/EBPβ pathway.
Mitochondrial ROS causes oxidative damage to lysosomes, lowering pH and activating AEP. ROS also activates C/EBPβ, which transcriptionally upregulates AEP.
Endfoot damage → AQP4 depolarization → loss of polarized CSF-ISF exchange → impaired glymphatic clearance of Aβ and tau.
Released GFAP enters CSF, then crosses into blood via arachnoid granulations and lymphatic drainage. Plasma GFAP correlates with CSF levels.
Released GFAP directly enters CSF. CSF GFAP levels are higher than plasma and more directly reflect CNS astrocyte damage.
Enlarged PVS indicates impaired glymphatic flow → reduced DTI-ALPS index. Both are MRI measures of glymphatic dysfunction.
N283-cleaved vimentin fragments are released from damaged astrocytes. Potential novel biomarker specific for AEP-mediated damage.
Active AEP cleaves intact vimentin at N283, consuming the healthy vimentin pool and generating non-functional fragments.
Full-length vimentin co-polymerizes with GFAP to form and maintain the intermediate filament network. Intact vimentin is required for network stability.
Intact GFAP-vimentin cytoskeletal network maintains astrocyte endfoot structure and tight apposition to blood vessels, keeping perivascular spaces narrow.
Normal narrow perivascular spaces enable efficient CSF-ISF exchange for glymphatic clearance. Enlarged PVS disrupts flow dynamics.
Healthy GFAP network and disrupted network are opposing states. As intact network capacity decreases (from vimentin cleavage), disruption increases.
Normal and enlarged PVS are opposing states. As endfoot integrity declines, PVS transitions from normal to enlarged state.
Glutamine synthetase is the primary ammonia detoxification pathway in brain. GS converts glutamate + NH₃ → glutamine, keeping brain ammonia levels low.
Reduced glutamine synthetase activity impairs ammonia detoxification, allowing ammonia to accumulate to neurotoxic levels. Similar to hepatic encephalopathy pathology.

Ammonia activates NKCC1 cotransporter in astrocytes → osmotic water influx → cell swelling. Also triggers calcium-dependent glutamate release and NF-κB activation, creating inflammatory amplification loop.

Ammonia causes endocytosis of mature APP from astrocyte plasma membrane and targets it to the endoplasmic reticulum (not lysosome). This is a novel amyloidogenic pathway distinct from neuronal APP processing.

APP in astrocyte ER is cleaved by resident BACE1 (β-secretase) and presenilin-1 (γ-secretase) to produce Aβ42 specifically — the principal component of senile plaques. First evidence that ammonia directly induces AD pathogenesis.
Chronic astrocyte swelling damages endfoot processes and contributes to clasmatodendrosis. Osmotic stress disrupts cytoskeletal integrity and membrane function.
GLT-1 (SLC1A2) is responsible for ~90% of synaptic glutamate clearance. When functioning normally, it prevents excitotoxic glutamate accumulation.

GLT-1 downregulation leads to extracellular glutamate accumulation. AD: 30-50% reduction in hippocampal GLT-1 (Simpson 2010). GWI: Impaired glutamatergic synapse function documented (Wang 2021). Gabitto 2024: snRNA-seq confirmed SLC1A2 downregulation in AD astrocytes.

Excess extracellular glutamate triggers excitotoxic cascade: 1. NMDAR overactivation → Ca²⁺ influx 2. Mitochondrial calcium overload → ROS production 3. Calpain activation → cytoskeletal damage 4. Neuronal death (necrotic or delayed apoptotic)

GWI CLINICAL EVIDENCE (Holton 2020): - n=40 Gulf War veterans, randomized controlled trial - Low-glutamate diet significantly improved pain and fatigue - Effect size d=1.16 (LARGE effect) - Mechanism: reduces dietary free glutamate while increasing protective nutrients
Glutamate excitotoxicity damages neurons through calcium overload, oxidative stress, and cytoskeletal disruption. Contributes to both acute injury and chronic neurodegeneration.

Aβ oligomers inhibit GLT-1 function and expression: 1. Direct inhibition of glutamate uptake (Li 2009) 2. Downregulation of GLT-1 expression (Mookherjee 2011) 3. Creates feedforward loop: Aβ → ↓GLT-1 → excitotoxicity → more damage

Infection triggers chronic immune activation in susceptible individuals. COVID-19: 10-30% develop Long COVID. ME/CFS: Onset often follows viral infection. Mechanism involves persistent immune dysregulation, not persistent virus.

Chronic immune activation → B cell dysregulation → GPCR autoantibody production. Same signature across ME/CFS, Long COVID, and AD. Chen 2024: IgG transfer from Long COVID patients causes disease in mice.
GPCR-AABs functionally dysregulate vascular tone: β2-AR AABs → impaired vasodilation AT1R AABs → inappropriate vasoconstriction ETA AABs → endothelin pathway disruption
Dysregulated vasoregulation leads to small vessel dysfunction and reduced capillary function.
Small vessel dysfunction leads to chronic tissue hypoperfusion, including brain.

Chronic hypoperfusion creates hypoxic conditions that promote: - Lipid accumulation in microglia (LDAM phenotype) - HIF-1α stabilization → glycolytic shift - Reduced oxidative metabolism → lipid droplet accumulation
Chronic inflammation activates IRG1 (ACOD1) → itaconate production → metabolic inflexibility.
Itaconate-driven TCA disruption shunts glutamate through GABA pathway.
GABA shunt generates ammonia as byproduct → astrocyte swelling → clasmatodendrosis cascade.

Vaccines induce beneficial trained immunity via epigenetic reprogramming: - Zostavax: 17% dementia reduction (no adjuvant) - Shingrix: 17-18% reduction (AS01B adjuvant) - BCG: ~45% reduction in bladder cancer patients - RSV vaccines: 29% reduction

Neurotoxicant exposure triggers the same chronic immune activation cascade as infection. GWI shows 14× higher dementia rate (9.8% vs 0.7%, Chao 2024). Mechanism: organophosphates → gut dysbiosis → systemic inflammation → neuroinflammation.

Cui 2024: Organophosphate exposure → Aβ accumulation in WILD-TYPE mice. Mechanism: Malathion → gut microbiota dysbiosis → kynurenine pathway → neurotoxic metabolites → Aβ. Proves environmental factors can trigger AD pathology without genetic predisposition.
Zhang 2026 (AJNR): GWI patients show significantly reduced glymphatic indices (Cohen

GWI-induced glymphatic impairment reduces brain waste clearance. Same mechanism implicated in AD progression: impaired Aβ and tau clearance during sleep. CONVERGENCE POINT: GWI, ME/CFS, Long COVID, and AD may all share glymphatic dysfunction.
Ferroptotic cell death releases polyanion cofactors: - RNA released from dying cells - DNA fragments - HSPGs from disrupted membranes These stabilize tau and Aβ aggregation.
Fichou 2018 (PNAS): Tau seeds are
Polyanions also promote Aβ fibril formation and stability.
Cellular dysfunction creates permissive environment for aggregation via cofactor release.
Yuan 2016: Microglia form protective barriers around plaques. TREM2-dependent recruitment → compact fibrillar core → sequester toxic oligomers. Dense-core plaques are PROTECTIVE, not harmful.
TREM2 deficiency → microglia cannot form effective barrier → Diffuse filamentous plaques (failed containment) → MORE neurotoxicity. Paradox: LESS plaque burden but WORSE outcomes.
Diffuse plaques leak toxic oligomers → greater tau hyperphosphorylation in surrounding neurons.
Dense-core plaques with intact microglial barrier show LESS surrounding neuritic dystrophy.
Estrogen acts via ERα/ERβ receptors to increase BDNF, promote spine formation, reduce APP processing, and enhance mitochondrial function.
Hormone replacement therapy restores estrogen levels. Critical window hypothesis: HRT within 5 years of menopause may be neuroprotective; later may be harmful.
Estrogen increases BDNF expression and promotes dendritic spine formation, enhancing synaptic plasticity.
Estrogen reduces amyloidogenic APP processing, decreasing Aβ production.
Testosterone reduces Aβ production and improves synaptic signaling in animal models. However, clinical trial benefits remain unproven.
In animal models, testosterone reduces Aβ production. Human evidence is weaker.
Chronic psychological stress activates the HPA axis: CRH → ACTH → cortisol release from adrenals.
HPA axis activation triggers adrenal cortisol release. In AD, hippocampal damage removes inhibitory control, leading to chronic elevation.
Chronic cortisol elevation enhances tau hyperphosphorylation and promotes tau pathology.
Chronic cortisol causes hippocampal atrophy and synaptic dysfunction, impairing plasticity.
Mindfulness, meditation, exercise, and social support normalize HPA axis function and reduce cortisol.
Low thyroid function (low T3/T4, high TSH) indicates hypothyroidism. Both hypo- and hyperthyroidism increase AD risk.
Hypothyroidism associated with 2-fold increased AD risk (Framingham). May contribute via reduced brain metabolism and impaired synaptic plasticity.
Intranasal insulin bypasses BBB to deliver insulin directly to brain. SNIFF trials showed memory improvement in APOE4-negative patients only.
Intact brain insulin signaling counteracts insulin resistance. Impaired signaling (Type 3 diabetes) reduces glucose uptake and Aβ clearance.
Melatonin regulates circadian rhythm, which controls sleep-dependent glymphatic clearance. Also has direct antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties.
